Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic because I had to work around my part-time job and limited resources. I'd usually carve out blocks of time on weekends and a couple of evenings when I knew I could really focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams and color-coded notes from the lecture materials. Some weeks I managed to put in around 26 hours, but it wasn't always evenly distributed throughout the week.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what shifts I had at work. I tried to keep Sunday afternoons sacred for studying, but deadlines would sometimes force me into late-night cramming sessions. When I had multiple assignments converging, I'd have to sacrifice attendance at actual lectures to catch up on the work itself. It wasn't ideal, but I made it work with what I had.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew an assignment was coming up or when I needed to download lecture slides before they disappeared. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post and that would remind me to check in. Honestly, it was often deadline pressure that got me to open the LMS rather than genuine curiosity, though I wish it were different.
Q4: For a while in the middle of the semester, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee before work. It became almost automatic, like checking my email. But then life got hectic with extra shifts and some family stuff, and that routine fell apart. I never quite got it back to that consistent level, but for those few weeks it felt natural.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by seeing things explained visually. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to sketch out concepts in my notebook. The discussion boards were also surprisingly helpful because seeing how other students approached problems gave me new perspectives. I appreciated when instructors posted visual summaries or infographics too.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings because I just didn't have the time or energy after working through the core materials. Some of the text-heavy PDFs felt overwhelming when I was already stressed, so I'd tell myself I'd get to them later and then never did. The optional quizzes also got ignored because they didn't count toward my grade and I had to prioritize what would actually impact my final mark.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I'd be assessed on or when it genuinely helped something click in my understanding. If I could finish it and feel like I actually learned something visual or practical, that made the effort worthwhile. Activities that felt like busywork or were just walls of text without clear purpose got pushed aside quickly.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With required work, there was this pressure that kept me going even when I was exhausted, but optional stuff felt like something for students who had more time and fewer responsibilities. I'd sometimes feel guilty about skipping them, but I had to be realistic about what I could actually accomplish given my situation.
Q9: Absolutely, I did so much studying offline that the LMS would never capture. I'd download the slides and videos to my phone so I could review them during my commute or breaks at work. I also borrowed textbooks from the library and made extensive handwritten notes because I couldn't afford to buy my own copies. A lot of my real learning happened with paper and colored pens spread across my kitchen table.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be working through problems and reviewing my hand-drawn diagrams for hours, but I never logged into the LMS once. I'd also study with a classmate sometimes, quizzing each other verbally or explaining concepts on a whiteboard. Those peer study sessions were incredibly valuable but they happened completely outside the digital system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download everything I could in one session because my internet at home isn't always reliable. Then I'd work through those materials over several days without needing to log back in. It was more efficient for me to batch my downloads and then study offline where I could focus without worrying about connectivity issues or data usage.
Q12: Around midterms, everything piled up at once and I hit a wall. I was working extra hours, assignments were due in multiple courses, and I started feeling really anxious about falling behind. My attendance dropped significantly during that period because I was trying to catch up on work rather than attending lectures I knew I could watch later. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me push through that rough patch.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work, I'd log into the LMS with good intentions but just scroll without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between watching a lecture video and completing an assignment, and the assignment always won because it had points attached. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections until I absolutely had to face them.
Q14: There were times I'd have the LMS open while I was too tired to actually process anything. I'd be watching a video but my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about bills or work schedules. I'd technically complete the viewing but I'd have to go back later and rewatch because nothing had stuck. It felt like going through the motions without real engagement.
Q15: I definitely avoided the more challenging problem sets when I was feeling overwhelmed. I knew they were important for really understanding the material, but the anxiety of potentially failing at them made me procrastinate. I'd tell myself I'd do them when I felt more prepared, but that perfect moment rarely came. Eventually I'd rush through them right before deadlines, which wasn't ideal.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, downloading materials and submitting work in concentrated bursts. Without a deadline, I'd often let days go by without logging in because other urgent things would take priority. It wasn't the healthiest approach, but it's how I survived the semester.
Q17: When I got good feedback on an assignment, it genuinely motivated me to engage more with similar content. Seeing that my effort paid off made me feel like I could succeed despite my circumstances. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and feel uncertain about whether I was on the right track. Quick, specific feedback made a huge difference in keeping me engaged.
Q18: The course structure with clear weekly modules helped me plan around my work schedule. Knowing exactly what was expected each week let me map out when I could realistically complete things. However, when the structure was too rigid with synchronous requirements, it clashed with my work hours and I'd have to skip those sessions. Flexibility in deadlines would have helped me a lot more.
Q19: Having video lectures I could pause and rewatch made engagement much easier for my visual learning style. The discussion boards were also designed in a way that let me participate asynchronously, which fit my schedule. What made it harder was when resources were only available for limited times or when participation required being online at specific moments I couldn't always make.
Q20: I think those students might be like me on my worst days, logging in and clicking around but not having the mental energy to actually produce work. They could be gathering information, trying to understand requirements, or dealing with anxiety that makes starting the actual assignment feel impossible. High activity doesn't mean high productivity, especially when someone is struggling with stress or competing demands.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity looks low because I do so much work offline after downloading materials. I'd watch videos once on the platform but review my notes from them dozens of times. My assignment scores were quite good because I put real effort into the work itself, even if my login frequency didn't reflect that level of dedication.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone is present and clicking through materials, and they can track submission patterns and deadline compliance. But they completely miss the quality of attention, the offline studying, the peer discussions, and the personal circumstances affecting engagement. They see the skeleton of participation but not the flesh of actual learning.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, making connections, and caring about understanding it rather than just completing tasks. It's when I find myself sketching out concepts on scrap paper during my work breaks or explaining ideas to my roommate. True engagement is mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged into a system.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I studied during my commute with downloaded materials, or the hours I spent creating visual study guides by hand. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions that helped me manage my anxiety enough to keep going, or the sacrifices I made choosing studying over sleep. It also can't show the determination it takes to succeed with limited resources while working to support myself.
